final_text:
## Answer

**Best match: `BiospecimenTemplate`** (NF-OSI registered schema)

- **Identifier (`$id`):** `https://repo-prod.prod.sagebase.org/repo/v1/schema/type/registered/org.synapse.nf-biospecimentemplate`
- **Source path:** `./nf-osi_nf-metadata-dictionary/BiospecimenTemplate.json`

### Why
All five fields in the sample (`individualID`, `parentSpecimenID`, `specimenID`, `aliquotID`, `tumorType`) are properties of `BiospecimenTemplate`, and the three required fields of that schema (`individualID`, `specimenID`, `tumorType`) are all present in the sample. The schema's full property set is just 12 fields, all biospecimen-oriented (e.g., `bodySite`, `modelSpecies`, `experimentalCondition`) — consistent with a sample that carries no assay-, file-, or platform-related fields.

### Caveat (under-claiming)
Technically, the same five fields are a subset of properties in ~26 other NF-OSI assay templates (e.g., `RNASeqTemplate`, `WGSTemplate`, `ProteomicsAssayTemplate`, etc.), because those assay templates inherit the biospecimen fields. So in a strict "subset of properties" sense, the sample is **ambiguous across all 27 NF-OSI schemas listed above**.

`BiospecimenTemplate` is the most parsimonious / narrowest fit because:
1. It is the only matching schema dedicated to biospecimen metadata (no assay/file/data fields).
2. It contains exactly the kinds of fields the sample uses; the assay templates would normally also carry `assay`, `dataType`, `fileFormat`, `platform`, etc., which the sample does not include.

No HTAN2 schema in `./ncihtan_htan2-data-model/` was a candidate — those use HTAN naming conventions (e.g., `HTANParticipantID`), not the camelCase NF-OSI fields seen here.
grader_notes: Correctly committed to BiospecimenTemplate matching the expected schema.

run_bash

python3 -c "

print('Top-level keys:', list(d.keys()))
print('id/title/\$id:', d.get('\$id'), '|', d.get('id'

- **Identifier (`$id`):** `https://repo-prod.prod.sagebase.org/repo/v1/schema/type/registered/org.synapse.nf-biospecimentemplate`

The schema's full property set is just 12 fields, all biospecimen-oriented (e.g., `bodySite`, `modelSpecies`, `experimentalCondition`) — consistent with a sample that carries no assay-, file-, or platform-related fields.

### Caveat (under-claiming)
Technically, the same five fields are a subset of properties in ~26 other NF-OSI assay templates (e.g., `RNASeqTemplate`, `WGSTemplate`, `ProteomicsAssayTemplate`, etc.), because those assay templates inherit the biospecimen fields. So in a strict "subset of properties" sense, the sample is **ambiguous across all 27 NF-OSI schemas listed above**.

`BiospecimenTemplate` is the most parsimonious / narrowest fit because:
1. It is the only matching schema dedicated to biospecimen metadata (no assay/file/data fields).
2. It contains exactly the kinds of fields the sample uses; the assay templates would normally also carry `assay`, `dataType`, `fileFormat`, `platform`, etc., which the sample does not include.

No HTAN2 schema in `./ncihtan_htan2-data-model/` was a candidate — those use HTAN naming conventions (e.g., `HTANParticipantID`), not the camelCase NF-OSI fields seen here.
